@graph.name: 9 Materials That Help With Commenting Skills
@graph.description: When your child watches the world but doesn't comment on it — when language is for getting things but never for sharing what they notice — these materials help build the skill of sharing observations that connects us all.

@graph.@id: https://pinnacleblooms.org/intervention-techniques/speech-language pathology / social communication / pragmatic language / expressive language/commenting / declarative language / joint attention / spontaneous communication / social sharing/9-materials-that-help-with-commenting-skills#faq

mainEntity.name: Why does my son is five years old and he talks. he really does talk. he can ask for things he wants — 'i want juice,' 'give me the ipad,' 'can i have a cookie?' he can answer questions when i ask them — 'what color is this?' 'blue.' 'what did you do at school?' 'played outside.' he can label things — pictures in books, objects around the house, characters in his shows. his vocabulary is actually pretty good for his age. his speech is clear. people can understand him. but here's what's missing, and i didn't even realize it was missing until his preschool teacher pointed it out: he never comments. he never looks at something interesting and says 'wow!' he never looks out the window at a dog walking by and says 'doggy!' with that excitement in his voice that wants me to look too. he never watches something happen and shares his reaction — 'that's funny!' or 'that's silly!' or 'uh oh, it fell down!' when we're playing together and something unexpected happens — a tower falls, a toy does something surprising — he doesn't remark on it. he doesn't look at me to share the moment. when we're reading a book and there's a surprising picture or a silly character, he just looks at it. he doesn't say anything about it, doesn't point, doesn't laugh and look at me. when he sees something he likes — a favorite character on tv, a cool truck driving by — he doesn't tell me. he doesn't say 'look, mommy!' all his language is functional. it's for getting things he needs or answering questions someone asks him. but it's never for sharing. never for connecting. never for saying 'hey, i noticed something and i want you to notice it too.' i watch other children his age, and they're constantly commenting on everything around them. at the playground: 'look, mommy, a bird!' 'the slide is so tall!' 'that boy has the same shoes as me!' at the grocery store: 'the watermelon is so big!' 'i see the cookies!' 'that cart has a funny wheel!' they're narrating their world, sharing their observations, pulling their parents into their experience every few seconds. but my son just... observes silently. he sees everything. i know he does — he's very observant, very aware of his environment. but he doesn't share it. he doesn't bring me into what he's noticing. it's like there's a wall between what he sees and the impulse to share it with someone else. at first i thought maybe he just wasn't a talkative kid. but it's more specific than that. he talks when he wants something. he talks when directly asked. he just doesn't talk to share experience. and the thing is, commenting is how children connect. it's how they build relationships, how they share joy, how they learn through back-and-forth about the world. without it, he's missing so much social interaction. without it, conversations never really get started. why doesn't my child comment? why doesn't he share what he notices, thinks, and feels about the world around him?

acceptedAnswer.text: COMMENTING SKILL DEFICITS / DECLARATIVE LANGUAGE DELAYS / REDUCED SPONTANEOUS SOCIAL COMMUNICATION / JOINT ATTENTION COMMUNICATION DIFFICULTIES / PRAGMATIC LANGUAGE CHALLENGES IN COMMENTING! Commenting refers to the use of language to share observations, reactions, thoughts, and feelings about the world — not to request something or answer a question, but simply to share experience with another person. Commenting is a form of declarative language (language that declares or states information for social purposes) and is closely tied to joint attention (the ability to share attention with another person toward a shared focus). Children with commenting skill deficits may have adequate vocabulary and the ability to request and respond, but lack the spontaneous use of language to share what they notice. This pattern is common in autism spectrum disorder and other social communication differences, where the social-sharing function of language develops later or requires explicit support. Commenting is foundational to conversation, social connection, and learning through social interaction — its absence significantly impacts social development and relationship building. Commenting is fundamentally a social-communicative act — it requires not just language skills but the understanding that sharing observations creates connection, that other people's attention and experience matter, and that language can be used not just instrumentally (to get things) but socially (to share experience). In autism spectrum disorder and related conditions, the social-sharing function of communication often develops differently. The child may develop requesting early (because it has clear, tangible outcomes) while commenting lags (because its 'reward' is social connection, which may be less inherently motivating or less clearly perceived). Intervention focuses on building the motivation and habit of sharing, creating high-interest opportunities that naturally elicit comments, modeling commenting language, and systematically teaching that sharing observations leads to positive social responses. Joint attention activities are foundational, as commenting typically emerges from established joint attention skills.

mainEntity.name: What is Commenting Skill Deficits / Declarative Language Delays / Reduced Spontaneous Social Communication / Joint Attention Communication Difficulties / Pragmatic Language Challenges in Commenting?

acceptedAnswer.text: Commenting refers to the use of language to share observations, reactions, thoughts, and feelings about the world — not to request something or answer a question, but simply to share experience with another person. Commenting is a form of declarative language (language that declares or states information for social purposes) and is closely tied to joint attention (the ability to share attention with another person toward a shared focus). Children with commenting skill deficits may have adequate vocabulary and the ability to request and respond, but lack the spontaneous use of language to share what they notice. This pattern is common in autism spectrum disorder and other social communication differences, where the social-sharing function of language develops later or requires explicit support. Commenting is foundational to conversation, social connection, and learning through social interaction — its absence significantly impacts social development and relationship building. Common signs include: Uses language to request but rarely or never to share observations, Answers questions but doesn't initiate comments, Doesn't say 'Look!' or point things out to share attention, Watches interesting events without remarking on them, Doesn't share reactions ('That's funny!' 'Wow!' 'Uh oh!').

mainEntity.name: How does a Cause-and-Effect Toys (Pop-Up Toys, Musical Push Toys, Spinning Tops) help with commenting skill deficits?

acceptedAnswer.text: Cause-and-effect toys create moments of surprise, anticipation, and excitement that naturally elicit comments. When a child pushes a button and something unexpected pops up, when a spinning top suddenly stops, when a musical toy plays a surprising sound — these moments create emotional reactions that are the foundation of commenting. The surprise factor ('Whoa!'), the anticipation ('It's gonna pop!'), the reaction to the effect ('It fell down!') — all of these are natural comment opportunities. Adults can model commenting language in these high-engagement moments when the child is most likely to be receptive and may naturally want to share the experience. Cause-and-effect toys turn abstract 'commenting practice' into genuine moments of shared excitement. Price: ₹200–1,500. DIY option: Any activity with clear cause and effect works: dropping things that make noise, popping bubbles, knocking down stacked cups, pulling cloths off hidden objects. The key is the moment of effect that creates something worth commenting on. Safety: Ensure pop-up mechanisms don't have pinch points. Supervise small parts. Some children may be startled by sudden effects — gauge the child's comfort level and adjust intensity.

mainEntity.name: How does a Visually Engaging Books (Pop-Up Books, Touch-and-Feel Books, Flap Books) help with commenting skill deficits?

acceptedAnswer.text: Interactive books create natural pauses and discovery moments that invite commenting. Pop-up elements create surprise ('Wow, look at that!'). Touch-and-feel textures create sensory experiences worth sharing ('It's so soft!'). Flap books create anticipation and discovery ('What's under here?' 'It's a frog!'). Unlike passive picture books where adults do most of the talking, interactive books give children something to discover and react to — something to comment on. The structure of the book (flap, then reveal) creates a natural rhythm of anticipation and reaction that mirrors commenting patterns. Adults can model comments during discoveries while leaving space for the child to fill in their own reactions. Price: ₹300–1,200. DIY option: Create simple lift-the-flap activities with paper and sticky notes. Hide pictures under flaps made from construction paper. Put interesting textures on cardboard 'pages.' The discovery element is what matters, not production quality. Safety: Pop-up mechanisms can tear with rough handling — supervise and teach gentle use. Ensure any small elements are securely attached.

mainEntity.name: How does a Bubbles and Bubble Toys help with commenting skill deficits?

acceptedAnswer.text: Bubbles are universally engaging and create continuous opportunities for commenting. Their unpredictable movement ('It's going up!'), their fragile nature ('Pop!'), their iridescent beauty ('So pretty!'), their silly behavior ('It landed on your nose!') — all of these are natural comment triggers. Bubbles create a shared visual focus that is the foundation of joint attention commenting. The repetitive nature of bubble play allows for multiple comment opportunities in a single activity, building the habit of sharing observations. Bubbles also create natural turn-taking (blow, comment, repeat) that structures social interaction. For children just learning to comment, the high engagement and visual salience of bubbles reduces the effort of finding something to talk about. Price: ₹50–500. DIY option: Homemade bubble solution (dish soap, water, and optionally corn syrup for durability). DIY wands from pipe cleaners, cookie cutters, or slotted spoons. The bubbles themselves are the engaging element, regardless of equipment. Safety: Keep bubble solution away from eyes. Supervise to prevent drinking. Bubbles on floors can create slipping hazards.

mainEntity.name: How does a Barrier Games and Shared Activity Sets help with commenting skill deficits?

acceptedAnswer.text: Barrier games structure communication by creating a need to share information. In a barrier game, two players have matching materials but can't see each other's setup — one player describes what they're doing while the other follows along, then they compare. This explicitly requires commenting: 'I'm putting the red block on top.' 'The dog goes in the house.' The barrier makes commenting functional — without sharing what you're doing, the game doesn't work. Shared activity sets (where both partners work on the same thing simultaneously) create similar needs. These games transform commenting from something 'nice to do' into something essential for the activity. The structure teaches that sharing observations is how we coordinate, collaborate, and connect. Price: ₹300–2,000. DIY option: Create barrier games with any matching sets: two identical coloring pages with a folder between players, matching building block sets, identical sticker scenes. A file folder or box lid makes an effective barrier. Safety: Standard toy safety applies. Ensure barrier is stable and won't fall on child.

mainEntity.name: How does a Photo Cards and Interesting Image Sets help with commenting skill deficits?

acceptedAnswer.text: Photo cards and image sets provide clear, concrete things to comment on. Photos of funny faces, unusual situations, interesting animals, surprising actions — all of these give children specific visual content to react to and share. For children who struggle to generate comments spontaneously from the complexity of real life, photos simplify the task by providing a bounded, static focus. Adults can model commenting language ('She looks surprised!', 'That's a huge fish!', 'He's wearing a silly hat!') while the visual reference is present. Photo cards can be sequenced from high-interest (funny, surprising) to more subtle (everyday scenes) as commenting skills develop. They also provide vocabulary support — seeing the image helps retrieve the words. Price: ₹200–1,000. DIY option: Create photo collections from magazines, printed images, or family photos. Focus on high-interest content: funny expressions, unusual situations, favorite animals, surprising scenes. Mount on cardstock for durability. Safety: Ensure images are age-appropriate. Be aware of any images that might trigger anxiety or distress for specific children.

mainEntity.name: How does a Sensory Play Materials (Playdough, Sand, Water Play, Slime) help with commenting skill deficits?

acceptedAnswer.text: Sensory play materials create continuous experiences worth commenting on. The texture of slime ('So slimy!'), the way sand pours ('Look, it's falling!'), the squishy feel of playdough ('It's soft!'), the splash of water ('Wet!') — all of these are immediate, embodied experiences that naturally call for sharing. Sensory play holds attention while providing constant stimulation that triggers reactions. The open-ended nature of sensory materials means there's no 'right way' to play — any action creates an effect that can be commented on. Adults can narrate alongside the child ('I'm making it flat,' 'Mine is blue,' 'It feels cold') modeling the running commentary that reflects shared experience. Price: ₹100–800. DIY option: Homemade playdough, kinetic sand alternatives, water with added textures or colors, simple slime recipes. Natural sensory materials like dry rice, beans, or shaving cream also work well. Safety: Ensure non-toxic materials. Supervise to prevent mouthing. Be aware of any sensory sensitivities — some children may need gradual introduction to certain textures.

@graph.name: How to Help a Child with Commenting Skill Deficits
@graph.description: A graded approach using evidence-based materials. Commenting is the social use of language — sharing observations, reactions, and experiences for connection rather than for obtaining something. When children have language but don't comment, they're missing the communicative function that builds relationships, sustains conversation, and shares inner experience. Intervention focuses on creating high-interest opportunities that naturally elicit comments, modeling the language of sharing, and building the habit and reward of social communication.

This pattern is common in autism spectrum disorder and other social communication differences, where the social-sharing function of language develops later or requires explicit support. citation.name: Research on Social Communication Development and Pragmatic Language
citation.description: Research in social communication and pragmatic language development indicates that commenting (declarative language used for social sharing) is distinct from requesting (instrumental language used to obtain desired items or actions). Children with autism spectrum disorder and related social communication differences often show a profile of relatively preserved requesting with delayed or reduced commenting. Evidence-based intervention focuses on creating high-motivation commenting opportunities, modeling declarative language, establishing joint attention as a foundation, and systematically building the habit and skill of sharing observations.

In a barrier game, two players have matching materials but can't see each other's setup — one player describes what they're doing while the other follows along, then they compare. This explicitly requires commenting: 'I'm putting the red block on top.' 'The dog goes in the house.' The barrier makes commenting functional — without sharing what you're doing, the game doesn't work. Shared activity sets (where both partners work on the same thing simultaneously) create similar needs. These games transform commenting from something 'nice to do' into something essential for the activity. The structure teaches that sharing observations is how we coordinate, collaborate, and connect.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create barrier games with any matching sets: two identical coloring pages with a folder between players, matching building block sets, identical sticker scenes. A file folder or box lid makes an effective barrier.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Standard toy safety applies. Ensure barrier is stable and won't fall on child.

item.name: Photo Cards and Interesting Image Sets
item.description: Photo cards and image sets provide clear, concrete things to comment on. Photos of funny faces, unusual situations, interesting animals, surprising actions — all of these give children specific visual content to react to and share. For children who struggle to generate comments spontaneously from the complexity of real life, photos simplify the task by providing a bounded, static focus. Adults can model commenting language ('She looks surprised!', 'That's a huge fish!', 'He's wearing a silly hat!') while the visual reference is present. Photo cards can be sequenced from high-interest (funny, surprising) to more subtle (everyday scenes) as commenting skills develop. They also provide vocabulary support — seeing the image helps retrieve the words.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create photo collections from magazines, printed images, or family photos. Focus on high-interest content: funny expressions, unusual situations, favorite animals, surprising scenes. Mount on cardstock for durability.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Ensure images are age-appropriate. Be aware of any images that might trigger anxiety or distress for specific children.

item.name: Sensory Play Materials (Playdough, Sand, Water Play, Slime)
item.description: Sensory play materials create continuous experiences worth commenting on. The texture of slime ('So slimy!'), the way sand pours ('Look, it's falling!'), the squishy feel of playdough ('It's soft!'), the splash of water ('Wet!') — all of these are immediate, embodied experiences that naturally call for sharing. Sensory play holds attention while providing constant stimulation that triggers reactions. The open-ended nature of sensory materials means there's no 'right way' to play — any action creates an effect that can be commented on. Adults can narrate alongside the child ('I'm making it flat,' 'Mine is blue,' 'It feels cold') modeling the running commentary that reflects shared experience.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Homemade playdough, kinetic sand alternatives, water with added textures or colors, simple slime recipes. Natural sensory materials like dry rice, beans, or shaving cream also work well.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Ensure non-toxic materials. Supervise to prevent mouthing. Be aware of any sensory sensitivities — some children may need gradual introduction to certain textures.

item.name: Video Modeling Tools (Social Videos, Commenting Demonstration Videos)
item.description: Video modeling shows children what commenting looks like in action — other children (or adults) naturally commenting during activities, sharing observations, reacting to events. For children who may not pick up commenting through observation in real-time, video provides a slower, more focused learning opportunity. Videos can be paused, discussed, and replayed. The child sees the whole pattern: notice something → make a comment → communication partner responds positively. Self-video (recording the child playing and watching it back) can also support commenting by making observations about their own recorded actions. Video modeling is especially effective for children who are visual learners and who benefit from seeing explicit examples of pragmatic language skills in use.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create simple videos of family members commenting during everyday activities. Record commenting during play and watch it back with the child. Find appropriate video clips showing children sharing observations naturally.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Screen exposure guidelines apply. Ensure video content is appropriate. Video modeling should supplement, not replace, real-life practice.

item.name: Communication Boards and Visual Comment Supports
item.description: Communication boards and visual supports provide ready-made comment language for children who have the interest to share but lack the spontaneous language retrieval to do so. A board with common comments ('Look!' 'Wow!' 'Funny!' 'I like it!' 'Uh oh!' 'Cool!') gives the child tools to express reactions without having to generate the language independently. Symbol-based commenting strips can be attached to play areas, books, or activity settings, putting comment vocabulary right where it's needed. These supports reduce the cognitive load of commenting — the child can focus on having the reaction and sharing it, rather than struggling to find the words. Over time, the visual support can fade as the language becomes internalized.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create simple comment boards using printed symbols or written words. Velcro-backed comment symbols can be placed at different activity stations. Even simple word cards ('Cool!' 'Wow!' 'Look!') provide support.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Ensure symbols are large enough to see and access easily. Laminate for durability. Avoid symbol overload — start with a few high-frequency comments.

item.name: Sabotage and Silly Scenario Materials (Missing Pieces, Wrong Objects, Absurd Situations)
item.description: Sabotage involves setting up situations that are 'wrong' or surprising in ways that naturally elicit comments. Give the child a cup with no juice in it — the 'It's empty!' is a natural comment. Put the wrong piece in a puzzle — 'That doesn't fit!' Hand them their sock when they asked for their shoe — 'That's not my shoe!' These engineered absurd or incorrect situations create mild, playful violations of expectation that are inherently comment-worthy. The child notices something is off and has natural motivation to remark on it. Sabotage is particularly effective because it creates authentic communication need — the child genuinely notices something worth mentioning. The comments emerge from real observation, not just imitation or prompting.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Sabotage requires no special materials — just creativity. Put a banana in the shoe basket. Wear glasses upside down. Hand them a spoon when they need a fork. The 'wrong' element creates the comment opportunity using whatever is available.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Keep sabotage playful and fun, never frustrating. Read the child's response — if they're distressed rather than amused, adjust. The goal is delighted noticing, not confusion or upset.

socialMedia.caption: He talks. He asks for things. He answers questions. But he never just... shares. Never says 'Look!' or 'Wow!' Never points out something interesting. Never brings you into what he's noticing. Commenting — sharing observations for connection, not requests — is foundational to conversation and relationships. Here are 9 materials that help: cause-and-effect toys, interactive books, bubbles, barrier games, photo cards, sensory play, video modeling, visual comment supports, and sabotage scenarios. Commenting can be taught. Connection can be built. Save for building sharing skills! 🔖 #CommentingSkills #SocialCommunication #PinnacleBlooms
